body_txt: It's common for characters to insult each other . But every so often, a creator will use their work to make a potshot at something from the real world instead. It could be another work, something political or religious, another person who the creator of the work dislikes, etc. Creators know that audiences won't appreciate it if their insult of choice comes from the narrator in a Non Sequitur , so instead, their potshot will usually take the form of a clever insult spoken by a character about their target, or an unflattering portrayal of their target. Whether it's because of a perceived rivalry, jealousy, bitterness, or pride, these potshots are usually derogatory and boastful. Sometimes, these potshots can be well-received. A "Take That" which is well-done and on-point can be enjoyable and satisfying to watch (even if mainly in a schadenfreude sense), and if the creator is particularly skilled, then even the target might be willing to admit that the Take That had a point, or at least was Actually Pretty Funny . Sometimes , however, the potshot will make the work or creator mocking the thing look bad, particularly if the critique is seen as hypocritical or hollow. A particularly nasty and/or unsubtle Take That can alienate fans of whatever it's targeting rather than making them laugh, or make the one mocking it seem like a jerk or a bully. "Take That" is something you hear a lot on DVD commentaries . Whenever the show gets to a gag that's at the expense of some real-life group(s), the commentator says, "Take that, celebrity / studio / organization / other target!" It is more likely to occur if the creators of the work are relatively confident they won't suffer any significant backlash from the insult; for example, someone who has Protection from Editors , or the writer of a Fan Work or Webcomic . Super-Trope of Biting-the-Hand Humor (the work makes fun of its publisher or network), Cable/Satellite Mudslinging (ads on satellite TV insulting cable companies and vice versa), Scapegoat Ad (blaming the employees/mascots of a competitor for its product's shortcomings), Strawman Product (mudslinging a competitor's product to uplift the one being advertised), and Take That, Audience! (self-explanatory), and a sub-trope of Bad Taste Call-Out . No relation with the British boy band (unless the author feels like it, of course), or with when "Take that!" is said between punches during a fight scene . If the "Take That" is directed towards themselves, then it's Self-Deprecation . Often uses Brand X or No Celebrities Were Harmed . Sometimes overlaps with Muse Abuse . Due to its intersection with Red scare, it could be the end result of Thinks Like Pulp Sci-Fi . Not to be confused with the band of the same name . For a full list of specific kinds of "Take Thats" a work can use, see Take That, Index! . Note to Tropers : Please do not pothole any "hilarious" insults you may feel like making towards your least favorite work to this article. We tried letting that slide once, but the potshots got too frequent for their own good and had to be quashed (that, and it's Conversation in the Main Page nine times out of ten).
